raw_text: Q3 Engineering All-Hands — July 1, 2024. Attendees: Alice Chen (VP Engineering), Bob Rivera (Platform Lead), Carol Kim (Design Lead), David Park (Infrastructure Lead), Eva Sharma (Backend Lead), Frank Liu (Mobile Lead), Grace Wong (Web Lead), Henry Adams (QA Lead), Ivan Petrov (Security Lead), Julia Martinez (Data Lead), Kevin Nguyen (DevOps Lead). Alice opened with company context: we hit 50,000 active workspaces last week, a milestone for the company. Q3 priorities were outlined: (1) Performance at scale — p99 latency under 500ms for all endpoints at 100,000 workspaces. (2) Multi-region readiness — evaluate and begin deploying to US-East primary, EU-West secondary, APAC-SE tertiary regions. (3) Enterprise features — SAML SSO integration, comprehensive audit logs, custom data retention policies, and custom domain support. (4) Security hardening — SOC2 Type II audit preparation for Q4. Alice assigned executive sponsors: Bob Rivera for performance, Ivan Petrov for security, David Park for multi-region infrastructure. All leads were asked to provide weekly status updates in the all-hands channel every Monday by 9am UTC.

Slack #infra — David Park: detailed provisioning plan for multi-region infrastructure. Phase 1 (July): set up Postgres read replicas in EU-West-1 (Frankfurt) and APAC-SE-1 (Singapore) using streaming replication from the US-East primary. All replicas will be provisioned as r6g.large instances with 100GB gp3 storage. Phase 2 (August): implement geographic routing via Cloudfront with origin failover — EU IP ranges route to EU-West API, APAC IP ranges route to APAC-SE API, all others to US-East. Phase 3 (September): evaluate active-active write capability with conflict resolution for the entity graph. Estimated monthly cost increase: Phase 1 = $8,200/month (replicas + egress); Phase 2 = $1,400/month (Cloudfront + routing logic). David owns all Terraform modules; Kevin Nguyen owns CI/CD pipeline updates for multi-region deploys. Alice Chen approved the Phase 1 budget. Ivan Petrov flagged that each region needs its own encryption key hierarchy with BYOK support via AWS KMS, managed by Ivan's security team. Timeline: Phase 1 fully deployed to staging by July 15th, production by July 31st.

Document: SOC2 Type II Preparation Plan — June 30, 2024 revision. Scope: Security, Availability, and Confidentiality trust service criteria. Audit timeline: evidence collection window August 1 through October 31, 2024; auditor is TrustPoint Advisory LLC. Key controls to evidence across five domains: (1) Access control — role-based access enforcement with least privilege, mandatory MFA for all human accounts, API key rotation policy every 90 days for service accounts, access review quarterly. (2) Change management — all code changes require PR with minimum two approvals, deployment to production requires explicit approval from the platform lead, automated rollback procedures tested quarterly. (3) Incident response — detection SLA under 15 minutes via automated alerting, initial response under 30 minutes, post-mortem required for all P1 and P2 incidents within 5 business days. (4) Data handling — AES-256 encryption at rest for all databases and file storage, TLS 1.3 in transit, key rotation every 90 days, data classification enforced at ingestion. (5) Monitoring — 24/7 automated alerting via PagerDuty, anomaly detection for access patterns, security event logs retained for 1 year minimum, quarterly security reviews. Primary owner: Ivan Petrov. Executive sponsor: Alice Chen. Budget: $45,000 for external auditor and tooling.

1:1 meeting notes — Alice Chen and Bob Rivera, July 2. Bob presented the detailed performance roadmap for Q3. Key projects and estimates: (1) Connection pool optimization — migrate to pgBouncer 1.22 in transaction pooling mode with a maximum of 200 connections per application shard, expected to reduce connection overhead by 40%. (2) Query optimization — add composite indexes for the top 10 slow queries identified during the April 10th incident post-mortem; each index addition to be done with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY to avoid table locks. (3) API read caching layer — Redis cache for GET /entities and GET /entities/{id} with a 5-minute TTL per workspace, invalidated on any entity write mutation; expected to reduce database load by 60% on read endpoints. (4) Worker horizontal scaling — target 20 concurrent worker instances by August 31st with consistent hashing for job routing to ensure workspace affinity and reduce cache contention. Bob estimates these four changes together reduce p99 API latency from 820ms to under 350ms at current peak load (10k req/min). Alice requested a staging benchmark report before each change is promoted to production. Bob will publish benchmarks to the #backend-perf Slack channel within 24 hours of each staging validation.

Slack #backend — Eva Sharma: the new connection pool configuration via pgBouncer is live in production as of 14:30 UTC July 3rd. Results after 2 hours of steady-state traffic: (1) p95 database query time dropped from 180ms to 55ms at peak. (2) Connection pool saturation dropped from 78% average to 22% average — peak saturation now 41% vs previous 95%. (3) Error rate on read endpoints: was 0.3%, now 0.01% (a 30x improvement). (4) Total Postgres connections: stable at 180 of 200 max during peak; previously was hitting 200/200 and queuing. The pgBouncer deployment uses transaction pooling mode with pool_size=40 per application instance, 5 application instances = 200 total. Eva also enabled statement-level logging for queries over 500ms in pg_stat_statements — this immediately identified 3 additional slow queries that do not yet have optimal indexes. Bob Rivera asked Eva to open Linear tickets for each slow query finding and assign to herself for resolution. Target: all 3 queries optimized before July 15th sprint end.

Meeting notes: Enterprise feature planning — Alice Chen, Bob Rivera, Carol Kim, Grace Wong, July 3. Discussed and finalized the enterprise tier feature set for Q3 delivery. (1) SAML SSO integration: supports Okta, Azure AD, and Google Workspace as identity providers; JIT user provisioning on first login; SCIM 2.0 optional; attribute mapping configurable per workspace; owner: Eva Sharma (backend API), Grace Wong (web UI), Frank Liu (iOS); backend target August 15th, UI target August 22nd. (2) Audit log endpoint: every API write action produces an append-only audit record with actor, resource_type, resource_id, action type, input payload snapshot, timestamp, and client IP; GET /audit-logs with cursor pagination (max 200 per page) and filters for resource_type, actor, and date range; owner: Bob Rivera; target July 31st. (3) Custom data retention: workspace admins configure retention window in days (30, 90, 180, 365, or unlimited); background periodic job soft-deletes then hard-deletes expired data respecting FK cascade order; owner: Julia Martinez; target September 1st. (4) Custom domain support: workspaces serve the web UI from a CNAME of their choosing via Caddy automatic TLS with ACME; owner: Kevin Nguyen; target September 15th.

Document: Audit Log Design Specification — Bob Rivera, July 4, 2024. The audit log provides a tamper-evident, append-only record of every write mutation at the API layer for compliance and security forensics. Database schema: table `audit_logs` with columns: id (UUID, primary key), workspace_id (UUID, foreign key with RLS), actor_id (UUID, foreign key to api_keys.id; nullable for system actions), resource_type (text enum: entity/claim/event/relation/api_key/workspace/extraction_run/integrator_run/integrator_action), resource_id (UUID), action (text enum: create/update/delete/revoke/trigger), payload (JSONB snapshot of the full input body received by the API, after deserialization but before mutation), ip_address (text, parsed from X-Forwarded-For with rightmost-trusted-proxy semantics), user_agent (text), created_at (timestamptz, server-side NOW()). Indexes: (workspace_id, created_at DESC) for cursor pagination; (workspace_id, resource_type, created_at DESC) for filtered queries; (workspace_id, actor_id, created_at DESC) for actor-scoped queries. RLS: workspace_id scoped, read access restricted to workspace admin role only. Retention: tied to the workspace's retention_days setting; the retention sweep job includes audit_logs in its deletion cascade. GDPR pseudonymization: actor_id can be pseudonymized via the DSR fulfillment process; the pseudonymous token is derived from workspace_id + actor_id via HMAC-SHA256 using the workspace-level HMAC key stored in KMS; the original mapping is destroyed immediately. Owner: Bob Rivera.

Slack #security — Ivan Petrov: mandatory MFA enforcement policy is now published and in effect. Policy details: starting August 1, 2024, all human user accounts with the admin role must have TOTP-based MFA (RFC 6238) enabled. Supported authenticator apps: Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password, Bitwarden TOTP. Machine accounts (API keys used by CI/CD, integrations) are exempt from this policy. Accounts not compliant by August 1st will be locked after a 14-day grace period (account can still read but cannot write). Ivan will send personalized email reminders to non-compliant account holders at T-14 days, T-7 days, and T-1 day. Grace Wong asked if CI/CD service accounts are affected — Ivan confirmed that all CI/CD pipelines use API keys (machine accounts, exempt from this policy), not user accounts. Alice Chen approved the policy formally. Kevin Nguyen confirmed all CI/CD pipelines already use API keys exclusively and are not impacted. Ivan will provide a compliance report showing the percentage of admin accounts with MFA enabled, updated weekly starting July 8th.

Sprint planning session — July 8, 2024 sprint kickoff. Sprint duration: 2 weeks, July 8–19. Capacity: 10 engineers at average 7 points per person = 70 point budget. Assignments: Eva Sharma: audit log database migration P0 (8 pts), audit log API implementation P0 (8 pts), connection pool monitoring dashboards P1 (3 pts). Grace Wong: SAML SSO web flow complete P0 (13 pts), dashboard performance optimization P2 (5 pts). Frank Liu: SAML SSO iOS integration P0 (10 pts), push notification v2.3.2 fix P1 (3 pts). Bob Rivera: audit log review and API polish P0 (5 pts), API v2 breaking change analysis P1 (5 pts). David Park: EU-West Terraform staging module P1 (8 pts), pgBouncer monitoring integration P1 (3 pts). Henry Adams: automation coverage from 83% to 85% P1 (5 pts), performance regression test suite design P2 (5 pts). Julia Martinez: data retention background job design doc P1 (5 pts), pseudonymization spec research P2 (3 pts). Kevin Nguyen: CD pipeline blue-green deployment P1 (8 pts), canary worker rollout P1 (5 pts). Ivan Petrov: MFA enforcement backend implementation P0 (8 pts). Sprint lead: Alice Chen. Velocity target: 65 points committed, 72 stretch.

Slack #data — Julia Martinez: data volume analysis for retention feature design, July 8. Analyzed production data distribution across all workspaces. Results: median workspace has 2,400 events, 12,000 entities, and 48,000 claims; p75 workspace has 8,200 events and 180,000 claims; top 1% of workspaces (enterprise tier) have over 500,000 claims, with the largest at 2.1 million claims. These numbers drive the retention job design constraints. Julia proposes a two-pass approach: Pass 1 (soft delete) sets deleted_at on l0_events older than retention_days, including a cascade to downstream derived data via database triggers; Pass 2 (hard delete sweep) runs nightly at 2am UTC, processes in 10,000-row batches with 100ms sleep between batches to avoid lock contention on production tables. Bob Rivera responded that the sweep job must also explicitly handle: vector_chunks (FastEmbed embeddings stored separately from claims), pipeline_traces (provenance data), and integrator_actions (merge audit trail). Julia confirmed she will add all these to the deletion cascade spec. Draft will be shared with Bob by July 12th for technical review before implementation begins.

Document: SAML SSO Integration Specification — Eva Sharma, July 8, 2024. Alaya integrates with identity providers via the SAML 2.0 protocol (OASIS SAML Core 2.0 spec, using HTTP POST binding). Supported identity providers in the initial release: Okta (tested with Okta Workforce Identity Cloud), Azure Active Directory (tested with Azure AD / Entra ID), Google Workspace (tested with Google Admin SAML app). Configuration stored per workspace in the new `sso_configs` table: columns workspace_id, entity_id (SP entity ID, format: https://api.alaya.io/saml/{workspace_id}), sso_url (IdP SSO endpoint URL), slo_url (IdP Single Logout URL, optional), certificate_fingerprint (SHA-256 of IdP X.509 cert), attribute_map (JSONB mapping IdP attribute names to Alaya fields). User provisioning: SCIM 2.0 provisioning is optional; default behavior is Just-In-Time provisioning — user record created on first SSO login using the mapped email attribute. Attribute mapping defaults: email → actor.email (required), displayName → actor.name (optional), groups → workspace roles (configurable mapping). Session handling: SAML assertion validity window maximum 8 hours; refresh requires re-authentication at the IdP. Security requirements: signed assertions required; assertion encryption recommended (AES-256-CBC); certificate rotation procedure documented in the security runbook without downtime (dual-certificate window). Owner: Eva Sharma (backend), Grace Wong (web), Frank Liu (mobile).

1:1 notes — Alice Chen and Henry Adams, July 9. Henry reported QA automation coverage is now 83%, up 5 points from 78% at the start of Q3. The remaining 2% to hit the 85% target consists of: SAML SSO end-to-end tests (depend on SAML sandbox environment availability, expected July 20th), data retention job tests (depend on the retention schema migration, due July 15th), and enterprise audit log integration tests (depend on the audit log API merge, expected July 14th). Henry proposed an automated performance regression test suite to run after every production deploy: a set of 20 representative endpoint scenarios against a fixed synthetic dataset, measuring client-side p50/p95/p99 latency and asserting they stay within 10% of a version-controlled baseline. Alice formally approved the initiative as a SOC2 Availability criterion evidence item. Henry estimates 5 days of work to implement and integrate with Kevin Nguyen's CD pipeline. Target: performance regression suite running in CI by July 25th. Kevin Nguyen will help with the CI integration piece — Henry to sync with Kevin by July 11th.

Slack #devops — Kevin Nguyen: CD pipeline v2 is deployed and in use as of July 10, 2024. Three major improvements: (1) Blue-green deployment for the API service — two identical API target groups in the load balancer; new version deploys to the inactive group, health check gate validates readiness (GET /health/ready returns 200 with all checks green for 60 consecutive seconds), then traffic switches via weighted routing policy (10/90 → 50/50 → 0/100 over 90 seconds); auto-rollback if error rate > 1% within 5 minutes of traffic shift. (2) Canary deployment for the worker service — 10% of worker instances updated to the new version; canary runs for 10 minutes; monitors error rate and queue consumer lag; auto-rollback if either metric degrades > 0.5% relative to baseline; post-canary full rollout if healthy. (3) Terraform plan output for all infrastructure changes is automatically posted as a GitHub PR comment requiring explicit approval from David Park or Ivan Petrov (both must approve for security-sensitive changes) before `terraform apply` can be triggered. Eva Sharma raised a concern: the canary worker split needs to account for job queue draining — in-flight jobs must complete on old instances before traffic shifts. Kevin will add a pre-rollout drain wait of 60 seconds and document in the runbook.

Incident post-mortem — July 5, 2024 write degradation. Severity: P1. Duration: 18 minutes from 09:12 to 09:30 UTC. Impact scope: ingest endpoint (POST /ingest/text) p99 latency exceeded 10 seconds for all workspaces; approximately 12% of ingest requests returned 504 Gateway Timeout; the worker pipeline continued to process backlogged jobs after the incident resolved, causing a brief queue depth spike. Root cause: a database migration deployed at 09:10 UTC created a non-concurrent index on the claims table using `CREATE INDEX` without the CONCURRENTLY keyword, acquiring an ACCESS SHARE lock that blocked all concurrent writes for the duration of the index build (18 minutes on a 45GB claims table). Detection: the Grafana alert for p99 ingest latency > 5 seconds fired at 09:14 UTC; PagerDuty paged David Park and Eva Sharma simultaneously. Mitigation: David identified the lock-holding process via pg_stat_activity at 09:23 UTC; killed the index creation process at 09:28 UTC using pg_cancel_backend; restarted index creation with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY at 09:30 UTC (completed without incident in 22 minutes). Root cause fix: added a migration linter to CI that parses migration SQL and rejects any CREATE INDEX statement without the CONCURRENTLY keyword; operator override requires a comment justifying the exception. Owner of fix: David Park. Preventive action: migration linter deployed to CI by July 9th; all new migrations now blocked from merging without the linter passing.

Slack #backend — Bob Rivera: audit log endpoint is now in review, PR #1523. Implementation details: GET /audit-logs with query parameters: resource_type (optional filter), actor_id (optional filter for specific API key), created_after (ISO 8601 timestamp for cursor-based date filtering), cursor (opaque base64-encoded cursor token), limit (default 50, max 200). Response structure follows the standard API envelope: {data: [AuditLogEntry], meta: {cursor, has_more, total_filtered, filter_reason}}. AuditLogEntry fields in list response: id, workspace_id, resource_type, resource_id, action, actor_id, ip_address, user_agent, created_at (payload field excluded from list for performance). Single record endpoint GET /audit-logs/{id} returns the full record including the payload JSONB. RLS is applied at the query level: the audit_logs table has a policy restricting reads to workspace admin role only (checked via the api_key scopes). Ivan Petrov is performing the security review; Eva Sharma is reviewing the query execution plan; Henry Adams is writing the integration test suite. Bob estimates the PR will be ready for merge by July 14th pending reviews.

Document: Data Retention Implementation Spec v2 — Julia Martinez, July 11, 2024. Updated to include pseudonymization scope per the July 9th 1:1 between Alice Chen and Ivan Petrov. Retention configuration: stored in the `workspaces` table as a new nullable integer column `retention_days` (NULL means no automatic deletion); workspace admins can configure via PATCH /workspaces/{id}; allowed values: 30, 90, 180, 365, or NULL. Background job architecture: implemented as a TaskIQ periodic task `job_retention_sweep` running daily at 02:00 UTC via the existing scheduler; uses a per-workspace advisory lock (same pattern as the integrator) to prevent concurrent sweeps on the same workspace. Deletion cascade order to respect foreign key constraints: (1) integrator_actions, (2) integrator_runs, (3) pipeline_traces, (4) extraction_runs, (5) l0_chunks, (6) vector_chunks (pgvector embeddings), (7) claims, (8) relations, (9) entities, (10) audit_logs, (11) l0_events. Batch processing: 10,000-row batches per table per workspace, with 100ms sleep between batches to limit I/O impact during business hours. Monitoring: emit structured log events `retention.sweep_started`, `retention.table_deleted` (with table_name and row_count), `retention.sweep_completed` with total_rows_deleted and duration_ms. Error handling: on exception, log `retention.sweep_failed` with workspace_id and error details; do not retry until next scheduled run. Owner: Julia Martinez. Target for staging: August 20th; production: September 1st.

1:1 notes — Alice Chen and Ivan Petrov, July 11. Ivan walked through SOC2 evidence collection progress in detail. Current status: 12 of 23 controls have complete evidence packages collected; 8 controls have partial evidence; 3 controls are blocked. Detailed blockers: (1) Control 3 — audit log endpoint not yet live; Bob Rivera's PR targeting July 14th; Ivan needs the endpoint URL in the evidence package. (2) Control 4 — key rotation automation not yet implemented; Ivan's own deliverable; target July 20th. (3) Control 7 — monthly vulnerability scan results needed; Ivan running the next scan July 15th; will have results by July 16th. Ivan raised a significant compliance risk: the current audit log design retains actor_id (an API key identifier that could be linked to a specific human via the api_keys table) for 1 year; this conflicts with GDPR right-to-erasure for EU workspaces. Approved resolution: pseudonymization using HMAC-SHA256 with a workspace-level key stored in KMS, making the actor link irreversible after DSR fulfillment. Alice confirmed the pseudonymization design is in scope for Q3 (not Q4 as previously assumed); Ivan to collaborate with Julia Martinez on integrating it into the retention job. Target: pseudonymization design spec draft shared for review by July 25th.

Slack #web — Grace Wong: SAML SSO web integration is feature-complete and deployed to staging as of July 12th. Detailed implementation notes: the web SSO flow uses the SP-initiated SAML flow; the user clicks 'Sign in with SSO' on the login page, enters their email or workspace slug, the frontend calls GET /workspaces/{slug}/sso-config to retrieve the IdP metadata, then issues a SAML AuthnRequest and redirects to the IdP SSO URL. On return, the SAML assertion is POSTed to /auth/saml/callback, validated by the backend (signature check, expiry check, audience check), and a session cookie is set. Tested scenarios: SP-initiated login with Okta (PASS), SP-initiated login with Azure AD (PASS), IdP-initiated login flow (PASS), expired assertion replay prevention (PASS), attribute mismatch error with informative UI message (PASS), certificate rotation without downtime using dual-certificate window (PASS). Known limitation: Google Workspace SSO sandbox account not available until July 20th — will test then. Pending: joint E2E session with Frank Liu for iOS↔web SSO consistency testing (scheduled July 18th), and security review from Ivan Petrov (requested, pending scheduling). Grace requesting Ivan's security review by July 19th to hit the July 22nd merge target.

Slack #mobile — Frank Liu: SAML SSO iOS integration is shipped to TestFlight build 2.4.0-beta.1 as of July 13th. Technical implementation details: the iOS SSO flow uses ASWebAuthenticationSession (Apple's recommended secure web authentication API) to present the IdP SSO URL in a sandboxed ephemeral browser session, ensuring IdP session cookies and login state are isolated from the main app's WKWebView. After successful authentication at the IdP, the session returns the SAML response via a custom URL scheme redirect: `alaya://saml/callback?SAMLResponse={base64url}`. The iOS app intercepts this deep link in the AppDelegate scene handler, extracts the SAML response, and POSTs it to the API /auth/saml/callback endpoint to exchange for an app session token. Security considerations: (1) the custom URL scheme alaya:// is registered in Info.plist and configured in the App Store metadata (Kevin Nguyen handling the metadata update); (2) the ASWebAuthenticationSession ephemeral flag is set to true to prevent cookie sharing with Safari; (3) the SAMLResponse parameter is URL-decoded and validated for size before forwarding. Pending: joint E2E session with Grace Wong on July 18th; Henry Adams regression testing on TestFlight. Frank notes that the iOS TestFlight build also includes the v2.3.2 push notification badge fix; Henry can test both simultaneously.

Document: Encryption Key Management Policy — Ivan Petrov, July 14, 2024 (approved by Alice Chen). This policy governs all encryption keys used to protect data at rest and in transit in the Alaya platform. Encryption at rest: all Postgres database volumes use AES-256-GCM via AWS EBS encryption with AWS-managed keys; all S3 buckets use AES-256 with SSE-S3; in-transit encryption uses TLS 1.3 minimum (TLS 1.2 with strong cipher suites as fallback for legacy clients; TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are explicitly disabled in Caddy). Key hierarchy for application data: master key (AWS KMS Customer Master Key, auto-rotated every 365 days) generates data encryption keys (DEKs) via envelope encryption; DEKs are rotated every 90 days and stored encrypted in the application config; raw DEK material never logged or persisted in plaintext. Per-workspace encryption: enterprise tier workspaces can optionally provide their own KMS CMK (BYOK — Bring Your Own Key); Alaya cross-account role assumption pattern; BYOK revocation immediately makes the workspace's data inaccessible. Audit trail: all AWS KMS API calls (Encrypt, Decrypt, GenerateDataKey, etc.) are logged to AWS CloudTrail and forwarded to the SIEM within 15 minutes. Incident response for suspected key compromise: (1) immediately revoke all active DEKs for the affected scope, (2) re-encrypt at-rest data using freshly generated DEKs within 24 hours, (3) notify affected workspace administrators within 4 hours with a description of the scope and remediation actions. Key access control: only the ECS task role assigned to the API and worker services can call KMS Decrypt; no human IAM principals have Decrypt access in production. Owner: Ivan Petrov.

Sprint retrospective — July 8 sprint end, July 19. Summary of completed work: (1) audit log endpoint merged to main, passes all security and integration tests — Bob Rivera. (2) SAML SSO web flow deployed to staging, all scenarios passing — Grace Wong. (3) SAML SSO iOS flow shipped to TestFlight, feature-complete — Frank Liu. (4) pgBouncer monitoring integrated into Grafana with connection pool saturation and query latency dashboards — David Park. (5) MFA enforcement backend implemented; admin accounts without MFA receive enforcement emails starting August 1st — Ivan Petrov. (6) Data retention design spec v2 published and reviewed — Julia Martinez. (7) CD pipeline v2 with blue-green API deploys and canary worker rollout — Kevin Nguyen. Items in progress: performance regression test suite (Henry Adams, target July 25th), EU-West Terraform staging module (David Park, target July 31st). Deferred: data retention job implementation moved from this sprint to the August sprint due to the pseudonymization scope addition (approved by Alice Chen). Retrospective observations: scope creep from the pseudonymization addition was identified early and properly flagged — a positive process example; the July 5th database lock incident exposed a missing migration linter gate, now fixed and in CI. Next sprint (July 22) velocity target: 70 points.

Slack #backend — Eva Sharma: slow query root cause analysis complete, July 20. Found 3 critical slow query patterns under production load: (1) GET /entities list view — executing a sequential scan with late-applied workspace_id and entity_type_id filters; missing composite index on (workspace_id, entity_type_id, is_deleted, created_at DESC). Current p99: 350ms at peak. After adding index (CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY): expected p99 under 45ms. (2) GET /claims list with entity_id filter — secondary filter applied after a full workspace table scan because entity_id is not in the index; missing index on (workspace_id, entity_id, created_at DESC). Current p99: 420ms. Expected after fix: under 30ms. (3) POST /integrator-runs/trigger active-run existence check — full sequential scan of integrator_runs to check for active (status='running') runs per workspace; missing partial index on (workspace_id, status) WHERE status = 'running'. Current p99: 180ms. Expected after fix: under 5ms. Eva opening Linear tickets now. Bob Rivera reviewed and approved all three index additions; each to be deployed as a separate CONCURRENTLY migration to avoid locking. Target: all three indexes in production by July 15th.

Meeting: Q3 performance benchmark review — Bob Rivera, Eva Sharma, Henry Adams, July 20. Baseline captured June 30, 2024 before any Q3 optimizations: GET /entities/{id} p50=45ms, p95=240ms, p99=820ms. GET /search p50=120ms, p95=380ms, p99=950ms. POST /ingest/text p50=95ms, p95=310ms, p99=680ms. GET /tree p50=210ms, p95=550ms, p99=1200ms. All measured at 10,000 requests/minute peak load. Current measurements (July 20, post pgBouncer + index optimizations): GET /entities/{id} p50=28ms, p95=95ms, p99=310ms. GET /search p50=78ms, p95=195ms, p99=410ms. POST /ingest/text p50=55ms, p95=145ms, p99=310ms. GET /tree p50=145ms, p95=380ms, p99=740ms. Q3 target was p99 under 500ms for all endpoints — already achieved for entities, search, and ingest; the tree endpoint at 740ms p99 remains above target. Bob proposed adding a tree-specific caching layer (entity hierarchy cache with 2-minute TTL, invalidated on relation writes). New stretch target proposed for the group: p99 under 200ms for all non-tree endpoints by September 1st. Henry to update performance regression baselines after 3 more days of steady-state data collection.

Slack #devops — Kevin Nguyen: EU-West staging environment is fully provisioned and running as of July 20. Infrastructure stack in eu-west-1 (Frankfurt): API service (2 x t4g.medium ECS tasks), worker service (1 x t4g.medium ECS task), Postgres read replica (r6g.large, streaming replication from US-East primary using WAL shipping), pgBouncer (t4g.small, transaction pooling mode), Redis (elasticache.cache.t3.medium, single-node), Caddy reverse proxy (t4g.micro). Replication lag monitoring: Prometheus scrapes the pg_stat_replication view on the primary and pushes the replica lag metric to Grafana; PagerDuty alert fires if lag exceeds 30 seconds for more than 2 minutes. Kevin's cost analysis: EU-West staging = $340/month (ECS tasks, RDS replica, ElastiCache, data transfer); EU-West production (to be provisioned August) = $4,100/month (larger instance sizes, 2 Postgres replicas for HA, dedicated pgBouncer cluster). David Park reviewed the Terraform module structure — approved with one change: Terragrunt should use a separate state file per environment to allow independent destroy operations. Kevin will update the module and re-apply in staging by July 22nd. Budget approval request to Alice Chen: Phase 1 production cost delta $4,100/month submitted via the quarterly budget process.

Document: API Key Security Policy — Ivan Petrov, July 20, 2024. This document defines the security controls for API keys (the primary authentication mechanism for Alaya's API). Key format: ak_{8-char-prefix}_{32-char-random-suffix}, total 43 characters. The prefix enables O(1) lookup in the api_keys table (indexed column). Generation: cryptographically random using secrets.token_urlsafe(32) in Python; the raw key is shown exactly once to the requester via the API response and never stored in plaintext. Storage: only the SHA-256 HMAC of the full key (using the server-side SECRET_KEY as HMAC key) is stored in the api_keys table; lookup is performed by computing the HMAC of the presented key and comparing to stored value. Scopes: read (access to GET endpoints for public and channel-level data), write (additionally includes POST/PATCH/DELETE mutations and private data access), admin (additionally includes restricted data, admin endpoints, and audit log access). Rotation policy: no forced expiry by default; workspace admins can configure optional expiry dates per key; service account keys used in CI/CD pipelines are rotated every 90 days via the automated key rotation service (Ivan Petrov's team). Revocation: DELETE /api-keys/{prefix} takes effect immediately and synchronously — the database row is deleted and subsequent requests with that key return 401 Unauthorized within the same request cycle (no eventual consistency). Emergency revocation: any admin of the workspace can revoke any key in that workspace; platform admins can revoke keys across all workspaces via the bootstrap admin endpoint. Key listing: GET /api-keys returns only the prefix (never the raw key); this allows auditing without exposure. Owner: Ivan Petrov.

Slack #data — Julia Martinez: pseudonymization design spec draft published for team review, July 21. Technical approach: when a GDPR Data Subject Request (DSR) deletion request is received for a specific actor (identified by their email), the process: (1) looks up all API keys associated with the actor's email in the workspace, (2) for each API key, finds all audit_log rows where actor_id matches, (3) replaces actor_id in each row with a pseudonymous token computed as HMAC-SHA256(workspace_id || actor_id, workspace_hmac_key) where workspace_hmac_key is a 256-bit key stored in AWS KMS per workspace, (4) immediately deletes the workspace_hmac_key from KMS (making the pseudonymization irreversible), (5) the actor's API key is revoked, and (6) a DSR completion record is written to the compliance log. Key technical notes: the pseudonymous token preserves the ability to count actions per actor-equivalent without re-identifying the actor; the HMAC key deletion makes the token permanently opaque; the process is idempotent (running twice produces the same token since the key exists until step 4). Ivan Petrov reviewed the approach and approved the HMAC-SHA256 scheme. Ivan added requirement: the workspace HMAC key must be rotated after any DSR completion (a new key generated before the next actor is pseudonymized in that workspace). Julia to incorporate this into the implementation. Bob Rivera confirmed the SOC2 audit log structure is preserved by pseudonymization — the action, resource, and timestamp remain intact, which is what the auditor needs.

Slack #infra — David Park: Postgres 16 production upgrade completed successfully, July 22 during the 02:00–05:30 UTC maintenance window. Actual duration: 3 hours 29 minutes — 47 minutes over the original 2.5-hour estimate. The overage was entirely due to the HNSW index rebuild on the entity_vectors table: the table grew to 2.1GB since the last estimate was made, requiring 130 minutes to rebuild (vs the 90-minute estimate based on the 50GB dataset figure in the runbook). All other steps completed on time. Post-upgrade validation: all health checks green, all smoke tests passing, no regressions in query execution plans (PG16's improved planner actually improved two slow queries by 12-15% without additional indexes). Connection count: stable at 185 max across all application instances. The runbook has been updated with the corrected HNSW rebuild estimate: 130 minutes per 50GB, or approximately 2.6 minutes per GB — operators should query pg_relation_size('entity_vectors') before scheduling the maintenance window. David also added a pre-maintenance checklist item: verify the HNSW index is not in the middle of an active ANN search query before initiating the rebuild. Alice Chen requested that the Postgres major version upgrade be scheduled as an annual maintenance event; Kevin Nguyen to add to the ops calendar for July 2025.

Document: Performance Regression Test Suite — Technical Design, Henry Adams, July 22. The performance regression test suite is integrated into the CI/CD pipeline and runs automatically on every merge to main and on every production deployment. Architecture: the suite uses pytest-benchmark and the httpx async client to send 50 concurrent requests to each of 20 representative endpoint scenarios over 30 seconds, measuring client-side latency. Endpoints covered: GET /entities/{id}, GET /entities (list with filters), GET /claims (entity filter), GET /search (hybrid query), POST /ingest/text, GET /integrator-runs, GET /tree, GET /health/ready, GET /audit-logs, POST /ask (informational only, excluded from regression threshold). Test data: a fixed synthetic dataset is loaded from bench fixtures at suite start into an isolated test workspace; the test workspace is created fresh on each suite run and deleted after. Baseline management: baseline latency values are stored in tests/perf/baseline.json, version-controlled in git; the baseline is updated manually by Henry Adams or Bob Rivera after intentional performance improvements (requires a PR with the updated JSON file). Regression threshold: p95 latency increase > 10% vs baseline triggers a test failure; p99 increase > 20% triggers a test failure. On failure: the CI step posts a detailed comment to the PR or deployment event with a table showing before/after p50/p95/p99 for each regressing endpoint. The /ask endpoint is reported as informational: p50/p95/p99 are logged but not asserted due to high LLM latency variability. Suite runtime: approximately 4 minutes; runs on a dedicated 4-core GitHub Actions runner in parallel with other CI steps, keeping total CI time under 12 minutes. Owner: Henry Adams.

Slack #backend — Eva Sharma: entity read caching via Redis is live in production as of July 23 at 15:00 UTC. Implementation details: the caching layer is implemented in the entity repository using a cache-aside pattern; on read, it first checks Redis with a computed cache key; on miss, it queries Postgres and writes the result to Redis with a TTL; on any entity write mutation (create, update, delete, claim create that affects entity metadata), it deletes the relevant cache key. Cache key format: alaya:entity:{workspace_id}:{entity_id} for single entity reads; alaya:entities:{workspace_id}:{type}:{access_level}:{cursor}:{limit} for list reads (cursor included to prevent the OOM bug from the July 20 Redis incident). TTL: 5 minutes for single-entity reads, 1 minute for list reads. Cache miss invalidation: explicit key deletion on write (delete before-write to avoid stale reads during write propagation lag). Performance results after 1 hour of production traffic: cache hit rate 73% for single-entity reads, 41% for list reads; p50 for GET /entities/{id} dropped from 28ms to 4ms; p95 dropped from 95ms to 12ms; p99 dropped from 310ms to 38ms. Database read QPS dropped by 58% on entity queries. Bob Rivera requested documentation of the cache key format and TTL choices in CLAUDE.md Production Config Notes. Eva will add the section by July 25th. Henry Adams will add a cache-hit-rate assertion to the performance regression suite baseline metrics.

Document: Worker Consistent Hashing Router Design — Bob Rivera, July 25, 2024. The TaskIQ worker fleet processes extraction and integration jobs. To reduce workspace-level lock contention and improve cache locality (the worker maintains in-memory workspace locks and caches), we implement workspace affinity routing using consistent hashing. Architecture: each worker instance is assigned a slot ID (0 to N-1) at startup via the WORKER_SLOT_ID environment variable; the total number of slots is WORKER_TOTAL_SLOTS (both set in the ECS task definition). A custom TaskIQ middleware intercepts job enqueueing and computes: slot = consistent_hash(workspace_id) % WORKER_TOTAL_SLOTS, then enqueues to the dedicated Redis queue broker:worker:{slot}. Each worker instance listens on its slot queue in addition to the global fallback queue. Consistent hash implementation: SHA-256 of workspace_id bytes → first 4 bytes interpreted as big-endian uint32 → modulo WORKER_TOTAL_SLOTS. This gives approximately uniform distribution with < 5% imbalance for typical workspace ID distributions. Workspace affinity rate: with 20 workers and random workspace IDs, expected affinity rate = 1/20 = 5% per worker slot, giving ~95% consistency (each workspace always hits the same worker, assuming no worker downtime). Fallback: if the target slot queue depth exceeds 1,000 jobs, the middleware falls back to the global queue to prevent deadlock during worker downtime. Monitoring: add per-slot queue depth and job processing rate to Grafana; alert if any slot queue depth exceeds 500 (50% of fallback threshold). Owner: Bob Rivera. Implementation: Eva Sharma. Target: 10 workers in production by August 1st, 20 by August 31st.

1:1 notes — Alice Chen and Julia Martinez, July 22. Julia presented the updated data retention implementation plan incorporating the pseudonymization scope. The background job will be implemented as a TaskIQ periodic task (`job_retention_sweep`) in the existing task registry, scheduled daily at 02:00 UTC via the built-in TaskIQ scheduler. For each workspace with retention_days set (non-NULL): (1) acquire a per-workspace advisory lock using the workspace_id as the lock key (matching the integrator's locking pattern in `packages/core/alayaos_core/extraction/writer.py`); (2) query l0_events with created_at < (NOW() - retention_days * interval '1 day'); (3) build the deletion plan respecting FK cascade order; (4) execute in 10,000-row batches with 100ms sleep between batches; (5) release the advisory lock. Pseudonymization hook: before deleting audit_logs entries, if the workspace has a HMAC key in KMS, run the pseudonymization step for all actor_ids in the to-be-deleted records. Alice raised a timing concern: for workspaces with millions of rows, the job could run longer than 24 hours (into the next scheduled run). Julia's solution: add a per-workspace maximum runtime of 20 hours; if exceeded, log `retention.sweep_partial` and stop — the next day's run picks up where it left off via the deletion window query. Alice approved this approach. Advisory lock ensures no concurrent sweep on the same workspace. Target: staging deployment August 20th, production September 1st.

Slack #web — Grace Wong: dark mode implementation is complete and deployed to staging as of July 23. Implementation details: the light and dark color token sets are defined in a single tokens.css file using CSS custom properties (custom variables with the CSS var() function). Theme switching: (1) the app respects the OS-level prefers-color-scheme media query as the default; (2) a user preference toggle in the account settings page stores the preference in localStorage and overrides the OS default; (3) enterprise workspace admins can set a workspace default theme via PATCH /workspaces/{id} (new theme field in the workspace config). The [data-theme=dark] attribute is applied to the HTML root element and overrides all custom property values defined in tokens.css. Carol Kim reviewed all color combinations for contrast ratio compliance — every text/background combination passes WCAG 2.1 AA minimum ratio (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text). Henry Adams ran the axe-core automated accessibility scan on 8 representative pages in both light and dark modes — result: 0 violations in dark mode, 0 violations in light mode (existing violations from previous audits are all in progress as separate tickets). Frank Liu confirmed that the iOS dark mode implementation is entirely separate: it uses UIKit's native dark mode appearance system (traitCollectionDidChange, UIColor.systemBackground, etc.) and does not consume the CSS token system. Joint validation between Grace and Frank scheduled for August 5th to verify visual parity on the shared design tokens.

Incident post-mortem — July 20, 2024 Redis OOM event. Severity: P2. Duration: 6 minutes from 16:42 to 16:48 UTC. Impact: entity cache hit rate dropped to 0% for all workspaces for 6 minutes, causing p99 for GET /entities/{id} to spike to 2,100ms (hitting the Postgres path on every request); no data loss, no ingest impact. Root cause: the cache key for GET /entities list responses did not include the cursor token in its hash — every paginated request generated a unique cache entry (the cursor is an opaque base64 token that changes with each page); over 4 hours of traffic, the number of unique list cache entries grew to 3.2 million, exhausting the Redis 2GB memory limit and triggering OOM-triggered eviction of all keys simultaneously. Detection: the Grafana Redis memory utilization alert (threshold 90% of maxmemory) fired at 16:43 UTC. Mitigation: at 16:47 UTC, David Park executed FLUSHDB on the Redis entity cache database — this cleared all entries and allowed the cache to rebuild from scratch. Root cause fix: (1) include cursor and limit in the list cache key (Eva Sharma, deployed July 21st); (2) reduce list cache TTL from 5 minutes to 1 minute to bound maximum cache entry count; (3) add a Redis memory alert at 80% threshold (P2) and 90% (P1) with automated OBJECT FREQ inspection to identify bloated key patterns. Preventive: added a cache key design review checklist item to the code review template for any PR adding new cache interactions. Owner of fix: Eva Sharma. Post-mortem completed by David Park.

Document: Entity Cache Key Design Reference — Eva Sharma, July 21, 2024 (updated post-incident). This document is the authoritative reference for Redis cache key formats used by the entity caching layer. Single entity read: key format `alaya:entity:{workspace_id}:{entity_id}`, TTL 300 seconds (5 minutes). Example: alaya:entity:d8f3a2c1-4b5e-4f6d-a7b8-c9d0e1f2a3b4:f5e4d3c2-b1a0-4f9e-8d7c-6b5a4e3d2c1b. Entity list reads: key format `alaya:entities:{workspace_id}:{entity_type_id_or_null}:{access_level}:{is_deleted_or_null}:{cursor_hash}:{limit}`, TTL 60 seconds (1 minute). The cursor value is hashed with SHA-256 and the first 16 hex chars used to keep the key length manageable (full cursor tokens are up to 200 chars). Access level included in the key to prevent cross-access-level cache pollution. Maximum cache value size: 100KB (enforced by the cache write path — values exceeding 100KB are not cached and served directly from Postgres; this prevents a single large response from consuming disproportionate cache memory). Redis instance: dedicated ElastiCache cluster (not shared with the Redis task queue instance) to prevent cache OOM from impacting job queue operations. Eviction policy: allkeys-lru (configured on the ElastiCache cluster) — on memory pressure, LRU keys are evicted before OOM. Memory alerts: 80% of maxmemory → PagerDuty P2; 90% → P1 (auto-page the infra on-call). Owner: Eva Sharma.

Slack #infra — David Park: EU-West production deployment is complete as of July 31, 2024. Infrastructure provisioned: API service (2 x c6g.large ECS Fargate tasks), worker service (2 x c6g.medium ECS Fargate tasks), Postgres read replica (r6g.xlarge, streaming replication from US-East primary, automated failover to the second replica within the region), pgBouncer (c6g.medium, transaction pooling mode), Redis ElastiCache (cache.r6g.large, multi-AZ with automatic failover), Caddy reverse proxy with automatic TLS (t4g.small). Geographic routing: Cloudfront distribution configured with two origin groups — EU IP ranges (GeoRestriction using the ip-ranges CloudFront service) route to the EU-West ALB; all other traffic routes to US-East. The routing is read-only for now: write mutations (POST/PATCH/DELETE) are redirected from the EU-West API to US-East via a proxy header and a 307 redirect. Write routing will become direct in Phase 2 (August, after the active-active write investigation). Cross-region write latency: measured at 95ms from Frankfurt to US-East for write mutations — acceptable for the current use case. Replication lag: 1.2 seconds average steady-state lag; alert fires at 30 seconds. Kevin Nguyen noted that EU-West monitoring has been added to the central Grafana dashboard with its own region tag filter. EU-West production costs are tracking to the $4,100/month estimate.

Slack #backend — Eva Sharma: worker consistent hashing implementation details published for review, August 4. The workspace affinity routing middleware is implemented as a TaskIQ middleware class `WorkspaceAffinityMiddleware` that hooks into the `on_enqueue` lifecycle event. Implementation: (1) Extract workspace_id from the job kwargs (all jobs include workspace_id as a string kwarg per the existing architecture). (2) Compute the slot: slot = int.from_bytes(hashlib.sha256(workspace_id.encode()).digest()[:4], 'big') % WORKER_TOTAL_SLOTS. (3) Check the target slot queue depth via `redis_client.llen(f'broker:worker:{slot}')`. (4) If depth < 1000: enqueue to `broker:worker:{slot}` (dedicated slot queue). If depth >= 1000: log `worker.slot_fallback` structured event with workspace_id and slot_depth, then enqueue to the global `broker:default` queue. Each worker instance listens on both its dedicated slot queue and the global queue (slot queue has higher priority via TaskIQ queue priority configuration). With 10 workers in production, load testing showed: workspace colocation rate 94.3% (6 out of 10,000 sampled workspaces had a job distributed to a non-primary worker due to fallback); queue depth distribution: max slot depth 847 at peak, average 89, well below the fallback threshold of 1,000. Deploying to staging August 5th; production August 10th if staging validation passes. Bob Rivera reviewed and approved the implementation. Load test report attached in the Linear ticket.

Document: APAC-SE Singapore Deployment Plan — David Park, August 3, 2024. APAC-SE deployment targets the ap-southeast-1 AWS region (Singapore), serving the Asia-Pacific and Oceania geographic regions. Infrastructure uses the same Terraform module as EU-West, parameterized for the new region. Key differences from EU-West: (1) Postgres read replica replication lag: due to higher geographic distance from US-East primary, expected steady-state lag is 180ms (vs 90ms for EU-West); this is acceptable for read workloads but write-path redirects from APAC will have 180ms overhead. (2) Regional Redis: APAC-SE gets its own ElastiCache cluster (cache.r6g.large, multi-AZ) because cross-region Redis latency of 180ms would make the cache miss path slower than a direct Postgres query; the regional Redis is seeded with entity reads from the APAC replica, not cross-region from US-East. (3) CDN: Cloudfront APAC edge nodes will serve static assets; API traffic routes to the APAC-SE ALB via Cloudfront origin groups with APAC IP detection. (4) Monitoring: APAC region tag added to the central Grafana dashboard; replication lag monitored separately from EU-West with the same 30-second alert threshold. Cost estimate: $5,400/month for production APAC-SE (larger Redis instance required vs EU-West, plus cross-region data transfer costs are higher from Singapore). Kevin Nguyen will provision staging APAC-SE by August 15th; production by August 31st. Owner: David Park; CI/CD owner: Kevin Nguyen.

(2) SP-initiated login, Okta, iOS: PASS — ASWebAuthenticationSession, deep link callback, token exchange, keychain storage. (3) SP-initiated login, Azure AD, web: PASS. (4) SP-initiated login, Azure AD, iOS: PASS. (5) IdP-initiated login (unsolicited assertion), Okta, web: PASS. (6) Session expiry (8-hour assertion validity) with re-authentication: PASS. (7) Attribute mismatch error — invalid email format in SAML attribute: PASS on web (informative error page with workspace contact); PASS on iOS (alert dialog with retry option). (8) Certificate rotation without downtime — updated IdP cert in Okta while 10 active sessions running; new sessions used new cert; old sessions with cached validation continued to work during the dual-certificate window: PASS. (9) JIT user provisioning — new user first login creates the user record atomically: PASS on web and iOS. Failures found and fixed during the session: iOS deep link crash on expired assertion (Frank fixed in-session); web redirect loop when workspace SSO config is missing the sso_url field (Grace fixed post-session, deployed August 5th).

1:1 notes — Alice Chen and Bob Rivera, August 5. Bob presented the worker scaling progress report. Current state: 10 worker instances in production with consistent hashing middleware active; workspace affinity rate 94.3% in steady state; job queue depth stable at average 47 jobs pending across all slots; p99 for cortex-to-write pipeline completion (for a 2,000-token event) is 4.2 minutes, down from 7.8 minutes at 3-instance configuration — a 46% improvement attributable to reduced lock contention and workspace cache locality. August target: 20 worker instances by August 31st. Infrastructure cost for 10 additional instances: $340/month (10 x c6g.medium ECS Fargate tasks). Alice approved the budget. Bob raised a new architectural concern: the integrator job (job_integrate) takes between 3 and 8 minutes for large workspaces (those with > 100,000 entities); at 20 workers, if multiple integration trigger events are queued simultaneously for the same workspace (possible after a large batch ingest), each worker will attempt to acquire the workspace integrator lock and fail — the failed workers will write status='skipped' for their run, which is correct behavior but wastes compute and creates noise in the integrator_runs table. Bob proposes: a per-workspace integrator semaphore in Redis limiting concurrent integration triggers to 1 active + 1 queued; any trigger beyond this returns an error response instructing the caller to wait. Design doc targeting August 12th; implementation targeting August 20th.

Slack #security — Ivan Petrov: API key rotation automation service is live in production as of August 5, 2024. Service architecture: a TaskIQ periodic task (`job_rotate_ci_keys`) runs daily at 03:00 UTC, querying the api_keys table for keys with the `ci_service=true` flag in their metadata that have created_at older than (90 days - 14 days) = 76 days (the pre-rotation window). For each expiring key: (1) generates a new API key with the same scopes and ci_service flag; (2) stores the new key in the GitHub Actions secret corresponding to the repository name (stored in the key's metadata as `repo` field), using the GitHub API with a service account PAT stored in AWS Secrets Manager; (3) schedules the old key for revocation 48 hours later (to allow in-flight CI pipelines using the old key to complete); (4) logs `security.api_key_rotation_initiated` with workspace_id, key_prefix (not the raw key), repository name, and expiry_in_hours. The 48-hour revocation window is implemented as a second scheduled task `job_revoke_expired_ci_keys` that processes a Redis-backed expiry queue. Currently managing 12 CI/CD service keys across 8 repositories. Ivan ran the first rotation cycle manually on August 8th: all 12 keys validated, 3 triggered for rotation (created 79 days ago), GitHub secrets updated successfully, old keys scheduled for revocation August 10th. Kevin Nguyen confirmed CI/CD pipelines picked up the new secrets without manual intervention. Grace Wong flagged that Vercel deployment API tokens are not yet managed by this service — Ivan scoping for Q4.

Document: Q3 Performance Benchmark Summary — August 5 snapshot, Henry Adams. Context: measurements taken at 15,000 requests/minute (50% above current production peak of 10,000 req/min) to test headroom. Test environment: the production cluster with 10 worker instances and EU-West read replica active. Detailed results by endpoint: GET /entities/{id}: p50=4ms, p95=11ms, p99=28ms (cache hit = 73% of requests; cache miss p99=88ms from Postgres). GET /entities (list): p50=18ms, p95=62ms, p99=145ms (list cache hit 41%). GET /claims (with entity filter): p50=22ms, p95=78ms, p99=190ms (no caching on claims yet). GET /search (hybrid query, 3-channel RRF): p50=62ms, p95=135ms, p99=290ms. POST /ingest/text: p50=38ms, p95=78ms, p99=145ms (API latency only; extraction pipeline is async). GET /integrator-runs (list): p50=14ms, p95=42ms, p99=95ms. GET /tree (entity hierarchy): p50=110ms, p95=280ms, p99=490ms (still above 200ms stretch target; tree caching on roadmap). GET /health/ready: p50=3ms, p95=8ms, p99=16ms. GET /audit-logs (list, no payload): p50=28ms, p95=72ms, p99=165ms. POST /ask: p50=1,100ms, p95=3,200ms, p99=6,400ms (LLM latency dominant; excluded from SLA). All Q3 committed targets achieved; stretch target (p99 < 200ms) achieved for entity reads; tree endpoint still at 490ms p99 (needs tree caching, planned August). Conclusion: the system has approximately 50% headroom above current peak before reaching the committed SLA boundary. Owner: Henry Adams.

Q3 engineering all-hands closing — August 8, 2024. Alice Chen opened with a summary of Q3 progress against the four priority areas declared July 1st: (1) Performance at scale — Q3 target of p99 < 500ms achieved ahead of schedule (July 23rd); stretch target of p99 < 200ms achieved for entity reads (38ms p99 with caching); search at 290ms p99; tree endpoint at 490ms p99 (just under committed target, tree caching in progress). The platform now has 50% headroom above peak traffic before SLA breach. (2) Multi-region readiness — EU-West production live since July 31st; APAC-SE staging deploying August 15th, production August 31st; Phase 2 (regional write routing) evaluation starting September. (3) Enterprise features — SAML SSO feature-complete in staging for web and iOS, targeting GA August 15th (backend) and August 22nd (UI); audit log endpoint live since July 31st; data retention job targeting production September 1st; custom domain support targeting September 15th; all three enterprise prospects aligned. (4) Security hardening — 20 of 23 SOC2 controls fully evidenced; MFA enforcement active for admin accounts; automated API key rotation live for all CI/CD service keys; vulnerability scan cadence established monthly. Q4 preview: SOC2 Type II audit window October 1 – October 31; dedicated isolated Postgres instances for Prospect C; GDPR pseudonymization full implementation; Vercel deployment token rotation; custom domain support; APAC-SE production. Alice formally thanked all leads for exceptional Q3 execution and noted that the team hit every committed deadline in Q3 (with one scope addition handled gracefully). Next all-hands: September 5, 2024.
